description: Effect.ts source-pattern rules harvested from Effect maintainer guidance (GitHub issues + the official Effect-TS skill). An agent checks progressively, fastest tier first: `oxlint` (instant, AST) -> `grep` (fast, regex) -> `review` (judgment, no linter can catch). Each rule cites its source.

tiers.oxlint: A custom rule in assets/oxlint-effect-rules/<rule>.ts catches it. Run oxlint with --plugin.
tiers.grep: A regex signal (`grep` field). Fast to scan; may over/under-match, so treat hits as candidates to confirm.
tiers.review: Semantic - the agent reads the `look_for` signal and judges. No linter can decide it.

rules.title: `class X extends Error` collapses structurally; use Data.TaggedError
rules.why: TS structural typing makes every bare Error subclass equivalent, so `A | B | Error` reduces to `Error` and catchTag can't discriminate.
rules.good: class DbError extends Data.TaggedError("DbError")<{ cause: unknown }> {}

rules.title: `Layer.scoped` was removed in effect@4; use Layer.effect
rules.why: In v4 `Layer.effect` accepts a Scope, so scoped acquisition goes through it.
rules.good: Layer.effect(Db, acquireDb)

rules.title: Schema.filter must carry a `{ jsonSchema }` option or JSONSchema.make throws
rules.why: A refinement requires a JSON Schema annotation.
rules.good: Schema.Int.pipe(Schema.filter((n) => n > 0, { jsonSchema: { minimum: 1 } }))

rules.title: Import from `effect/Schema`, not the deprecated `@effect/schema`
rules.good: import * as Schema from "effect/Schema"

rules.title: Use runFork/runMain as the entrypoint; runSync is an edge case
rules.why: runSync throws on any async boundary; it is for environments where async is genuinely impossible.
rules.bad: Effect.runSync(program)  // throws if program ever suspends
rules.good: Effect.runFork(program)  // or runMain from the platform package

rules.title: `Schema.optional` gives `T | undefined`; for an optional struct property use `Schema.optionalKey`
rules.why: `optional` adds `| undefined` to the value; `optionalKey` makes the KEY optional (exact optional property). Confirm which you meant.
rules.good: Schema.Struct({ search: Schema.optionalKey(Schema.String) })

rules.title: `Fiber.join` inherits all FiberRefs (incl Scope); use `Fiber.await` for value-only
rules.why: join overwrites the current Scope with the joined fiber's, which can fire finalizers unexpectedly.
rules.good: const exit = yield* Fiber.await(fiber)  // Exit value only, no FiberRef inheritance

rules.title: `Stream<A, E, Scope>` is a smell - a Stream owns its Scope; use `Stream.scoped`
rules.why: Streams are pull-based and manage their own Scope; Scope in R disconnects the stream's lifecycle from your outer scope.
rules.bad: const s: Stream.Stream<A, E, Scope> = Stream.fromEffect(Effect.acquireRelease(open, close))
rules.good: const s: Stream.Stream<A, E> = Stream.scoped(Effect.acquireRelease(open, close))

rules.title: Never `Effect.provide(AppLayer)` per request - use a ManagedRuntime or toWebHandlerLayer
rules.look_for: An Effect.provide(<a full app/service layer>) inside an HTTP request handler, route, or a runPromise/runSync called once per request. It rebuilds AND tears down the layer (DB pools, connections) every request.
rules.good:
const runtime = ManagedRuntime.make(AppLayer)
const handler = (req) => runtime.runPromise(program)  // or HttpApp.toWebHandlerLayer(app, AppLayer)

rules.title: Call a layer factory ONCE and bind to a const - calling it twice breaks memoization
rules.look_for: A function that returns a Layer (a layer factory) invoked more than once - e.g. `Layer.mergeAll(makeDbLayer(), makeDbLayer())` or the same factory called in two compositions. Each call is a distinct reference, so Effect memoizes them separately (two DB pools).
rules.good:
const DbLayer = makeDbLayer()
const AppLayer = Layer.mergeAll(DbLayer, OtherLayer)

rules.title: Don't `Effect.provide` inside a service method - receive deps via `yield*` at construction
rules.look_for: Effect.provide(<a service's Default layer>) inside a service method / business-logic function, instead of acquiring the dependency with `yield* Dep` when the service is constructed and composing layers at the boundary.
rules.good:
const inc1 = yield* Inc1
return { inc2: (x) => inc1.inc(x) }  // compose: Inc2.Default.pipe(Layer.provideMerge(Inc1.Default))

rules.title: Service property names must not shadow Tag built-ins (context/of/key/identifier)
rules.look_for: An Effect.Service whose returned object has a property named `context`, `of`, `key`, or `identifier` - these collide with the Tag class static members and the accessor proxy silently returns the wrong value.
rules.good: Effect.Service<S>()("S", { effect: Effect.succeed({ ctx: value }) })

rules.title: Service accessors are proxies - they break `flow()`; capture the instance or use `pipe`
rules.look_for: `flow(Service.method, ...)` using a service accessor point-free - accessors are Proxy-based and lose `this`, throwing at runtime.
rules.good:
const inc1 = yield* Inc1
const t = flow(inc1.inc, Effect.andThen(inc1.inc))

rules.title: Construct resources in dependency order - finalizers run LIFO
rules.look_for: A scoped block where a consumer (e.g. a FiberSet/fibers using a pool) is created BEFORE the resource it depends on (the pool). LIFO teardown then closes the resource while consumers still run.
rules.bad:
const set = yield* FiberSet.make()
const pool = yield* WorkerPool.make(...)
rules.good:
const pool = yield* WorkerPool.make(...)
const set = yield* FiberSet.make()  // pool outlives the set

rules.title: `Effect.fork` + `awaitAllChildren` races; prefer `forkScoped` for guaranteed teardown
rules.look_for: `Effect.fork(...)` followed by `Effect.awaitAllChildren` in the same synchronous block (fiber may not have started yet), or any long-lived fork without scoped cleanup.
rules.bad:
yield* Effect.fork(longRunning)
yield* Effect.awaitAllChildren  // may hang/miss it
rules.good: yield* Effect.forkScoped(longRunning)  // cleaned up at scope close

rules.title: Streaming HTTP/SSE cleanup belongs in the Stream, not the handler scope
rules.look_for: `Effect.addFinalizer`/`forkScoped` in an HTTP handler that returns a streaming response. Bun (and similar) consume the body after the handler scope closes, so the finalizer fires too early.
rules.good: const body = myStream.pipe(Stream.ensuring(cleanup))  // or Stream.unwrapScoped(...)

rules.title: Browser/edge config: build a ConfigProvider.fromMap (process.env isn't dynamic there)
rules.look_for: Config.* reads in a browser / Cloudflare Worker / bundled context relying on dynamic `process.env` access, which the bundler can't provide at runtime.
rules.bad: yield* Config.string("API_URL")  // undefined in the browser bundle
rules.good: Layer.setConfigProvider(ConfigProvider.fromMap(new Map([["API_URL", process.env.NEXT_PUBLIC_API_URL!]])))

rules.title: Contextual descriptions go on `Schema.propertySignature`, not `.annotations()` on the schema
rules.look_for: `.annotations({ description / missingMessage })` applied to a schema sitting in a Struct field. For transform schemas (DateFromString etc.) JSONSchema.make ignores it; the annotation belongs on the property signature.
rules.good: Schema.Struct({ createdAt: Schema.propertySignature(Schema.DateFromString).annotations({ description: "..." }) })

rules.title: `Schema.Class` IS a transformation - `Schema.extend` rejects it; extend the encoded sides
rules.look_for: `Schema.extend` applied to a `Schema.Class` (which implicitly defines a Struct->instance transformation). extend rejects transformations.
rules.good: Schema.encodedSchema(ClassA).pipe(Schema.extend(Schema.encodedSchema(ClassB)))

rules.title: `Schema.transform` drops brands on the encoded side - wrap in `Schema.typeSchema` to keep them
rules.look_for: A `Schema.transform` whose `from` (encoded) side is a branded schema where you expect the brand to survive - by default the encoded side is unbranded.
rules.bad: Schema.transform(BrandedString, To, { ... })  // encoded side: plain string
rules.good: Schema.transform(Schema.typeSchema(BrandedString), To, { ... })

rules.title: `Schema.declare` is for new opaque types; use `Schema.transform` for conversions
rules.look_for: `Schema.declare` used to convert one type to another (e.g. string -> HashMap). declare is for registering a brand-new data type whose decode/encode return the SAME type.
rules.bad: Schema.declare(..., { decode: parse, encode: serialize })  // for a conversion
rules.good: Schema.transform(Schema.String, Schema.declare(...), { decode: parse, encode: serialize })

rules.title: `Effect.tryPromise` treats a synchronous throw as a typed fail, not a defect
rules.look_for: Reliance on a sync throw inside `Effect.tryPromise(fn)` becoming a defect - it becomes a typed fail (matching Effect.try). Wrap in `async () => syncFn()` if you want a defect.
rules.good: Effect.tryPromise(async () => syncThrower())  // sync throw -> Defect, if that's intended

rules.title: Use `Schema.Struct` + interface for shared DTOs; `Schema.Class` only for identity-bearing domain objects
rules.look_for: A `Schema.Class` used for a plain public/shared data type that travels across packages or is used as a LayerMap/Data.equal/hash key. v4 structurally compares plain records; Schema.Class adds nominal identity that breaks that dedup. Reserve Schema.Class for types with behavior statics / true identity.
rules.bad: export class Prompt extends Schema.Class<Prompt>("Prompt")({ text: Schema.String }) {}
rules.good:
export interface Prompt extends Schema.Schema.Type<typeof Prompt> {}
export const Prompt = Schema.Struct({ text: Schema.String }).annotate({ identifier: "Prompt" })

rules.title: Delete re-export-only schema modules; import the canonical schema directly
rules.look_for: A module whose only job is to re-export schemas defined elsewhere (a forwarding facade). It adds an indirection hop that obscures where identity lives. Keep behavior-bearing modules; delete pure schema re-export shims and import the source directly.
rules.bad:
// schemas.ts
export { Session, Message } from "./core/session.ts"  // re-export only
rules.good: // import canonical schemas directly from their source module
